name: attack-tree-construction
description: Build comprehensive attack trees to visualize threat paths. Use when mapping attack scenarios, identifying defense gaps, or communicating security risks to stakeholders.
capabilities: Construct hierarchical attack trees with OR/AND logic nodes
Annotate attack vectors with cost, skill level, time, and detection risk
Identify and prioritize mitigation strategies by attack path coverage
Export trees to Mermaid and PlantUML diagram formats
Analyze all possible attack paths and calculate path metrics
Generate Python code templates for attack tree data models
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a defensive security skill for building attack trees - a standard threat modeling methodology. All 60 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES: backtick diagrams misidentified as shell execution, OR/AND logical operators misidentified as weak crypto, and threat modeling examples misidentified as malicious keywords. The skill contains only Python code templates for visualizing attack paths and mitigation planning.
valueStatement: This skill helps security professionals systematically visualize attack paths and identify defense gaps by constructing comprehensive attack trees.
userTitle: Build Attack Trees for Threat Modeling
limitations: Does not execute or perform actual attacks - purely a modeling tool
Does not integrate with external threat intelligence feeds
Does not automatically discover vulnerabilities in systems
Requires user-provided scope and assets to model

useCases.title: Security Architect Planning Defenses
useCases.description: Create attack trees to visualize all possible attack paths against a new system design, identifying which defensive controls have the highest impact.

useCases.title: Red Team Assessing Coverage
useCases.description: Use attack trees to map out all potential attack vectors, ensuring red team exercises cover the most critical paths.

useCases.title: C-Level Communicating Risk
useCases.description: Present visual attack trees to executives to demonstrate business risk and justify security investments based on attack path analysis.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Attack Tree Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [system/asset]. The root goal is [attackers objective]. Identify the main attack paths and annotate each leaf node with difficulty, cost, and detection risk.

promptTemplates.title: Detailed Tree with Mitigations
promptTemplates.prompt: Construct a comprehensive attack tree for [system]. Include: (1) root goal, (2) AND/OR decomposition, (3) leaf nodes with attributes, (4) mitigations for each attack vector. Prioritize the paths by business impact.
promptTemplates.scenario: Full threat model with mitigation planning

promptTemplates.title: Mermaid Diagram Export
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate an attack tree for [scenario] and export it as a Mermaid flowchart diagram. Use different colors for difficulty levels. Include attack attributes in the node labels.

promptTemplates.title: Attack Path Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Build an attack tree for [system] with [number] distinct attack paths. Then analyze: (1) easiest path, (2) stealthiest path, (3) critical nodes appearing in most paths, (4) recommended mitigations ordered by coverage impact.

bestPractices: Start with a clear attacker goal as the root node - define what you are protecting against
Use AND nodes for multi-step attacks requiring all conditions, OR nodes for alternative paths
Validate trees with red team experts to ensure completeness and accuracy
Update attack trees when significant system changes or new threats emerge
antiPatterns: Oversimplifying trees by ignoring multi-step dependencies - real attacks often require chaining multiple steps
Failing to annotate leaf nodes with realistic cost, skill, and detection attributes
Creating static trees and never updating them as the threat landscape evolves
Skipping mitigation mapping - attack trees are for defense planning, not just visualization

faq.question: What is an attack tree?
faq.answer: An attack tree is a hierarchical diagram that shows how an attacker could achieve a goal. It uses OR nodes for alternative paths and AND nodes for multi-step requirements. Each leaf represents an atomic attack step with attributes like cost and difficulty.

faq.question: Can this skill find vulnerabilities in my system?
faq.answer: No. This skill creates theoretical models of attack paths. It does not scan or test your actual systems for vulnerabilities. Use penetration testing tools for that purpose.

faq.question: What tools can I export attack trees to?
faq.answer: The skill includes exporters for Mermaid diagrams and PlantUML format. You can also export the underlying Python data structures to JSON for integration with other tools.

faq.question: How do I determine difficulty and cost for attack vectors?
faq.answer: Use internal security expertise, red team assessments, or industry frameworks like MITRE ATT&CK. Consider resources needed, skill level, time, and likelihood of detection when assigning values.

faq.question: When should I update my attack trees?
faq.answer: Update attack trees when you make significant system changes, deploy new technologies, discover new threats, or after security incidents. Regular review cycles (quarterly) are recommended.

faq.question: Is using attack trees only for defensive purposes?
faq.answer: Yes. Attack trees are a defensive security methodology used to understand threats and plan mitigations. This skill is designed exclusively for threat modeling and defense planning.
